1.description: Proxy advisors and information flows This figure shows how proxy advisory firms help with communication and voting between shareholders and firms. Panel A shows how proxy advisors talk to investors and make recommendations for voting. In step 1, proxy advisors have two-way communication with investors, which helps inform their recommendations. In step 2, shareholders vote on say-on-pay (SOP), but the information content of this vote is coarse as investors can only vote yes, no, or abstain. Panel B shows how, if a firm gets an SOP vote less than 70%, the proxy advisor (ISS) encourages the firm to talk to investors and disclose information about how it addressed shareholder concerns (step 3). We test whether firms respond to pressure by ISS to engage more in direct, two-way communication with investors (step 4).

2.description: Time trends in shareholder engagement This figure graphs our primary measures of shareholder engagement for the full universe of firms in the ISS Voting Analytics database with say-on-pay votes over the proxy years 2011 to 2019. Bars represent the yearly sample average for the Engagement indicator (left y-axis). Line depicts the yearly average of the Engagement count (right y-axis). We define variables in  Appendix C.

3.description: ISS survey on shareholder engagement This figure charts investor and firm responses to a say-on-pay (SOP) survey conducted by Institutional Shareholder Services (ISS) in 2011. The survey asked investors and firms, “At what level of opposition on a say-on-pay proposal should there be an explicit response from the board regarding improvements on pay practices?” We tabulate responses across five thresholds on the x-axis and graph the percentage of investors and firms responding with support for each SOP voting threshold on they-axis. See ISS (2011).

4.description: Distribution of say-on-pay voting support This figure presents a histogram of the percentage of shareholders approving say-on-pay (SOP) using the ISS definition of SOP voting support. Panel A presents the density of firms with voting support between 50% and 90%, while panel B shows the density of firms with voting support between 67.5% and 72.5%.

5.description: Regression discontinuity plots of shareholder engagement This figure presents the regression discontinuity plots of the Engagement indicator (panel A) and Engagement count (panel B) in year t+1. Each circle depicts an evenly spaced bin with a bandwidth of ±2.5% around the 70% say-on-pay (SOP) voting support threshold. ISS-treated firms have SOP voting support between 67.50% and 69.99%, while control firms have SOP voting support between 70.00% and 72.50%. The gray-shaded region depicts the 95% confidence interval. The solid line fits a first-order polynomial to estimate the relation between SOP voting support and shareholder engagement. We define variables in  Appendix C.

6.description: ISS treatment effect and shareholder engagement over time This figure displays the staggered difference-in-difference estimates for the ISS treatment effect over time, following the methodology of Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (2024). ISS treated firms have SOP voting support between 67.50% and 69.99%, while control firms have SOP voting support between 70.00% and 72.50%. Squares represent the preassignment differences for ISS treated firms during the 4 years before the low SOP vote. Circles represent the estimated treatment effects for the 4 years after treatment. The shaded region depicts the 95% confidence interval for each yearly coefficient. Panel A examines the Engagement indicator, and panel B focuses on Engagement count. Detailed results are provided in Table IA-9 of the Internet Appendix.
